policy_statement: Include carbon footprint and eco-efficiency criteria in quality-based plot conveyance competitions.
mechanism_description: It will include carbon footprint and eco-efficiency criteria in plot conveyance competitions so selected construction projects tend to have lower emissions.
primary_causal_pathway: Procurement criteria → lower-embodied/operational-carbon construction choices → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail: (1) Change: Public (or city-controlled) plot conveyance and tender evaluation incorporates carbon footprint/eco-efficiency scoring, shifting developer and designer decisions toward lower-carbon materials (e.g., low-clinker cement, recycled steel, timber where appropriate), efficient construction methods, and potentially lower-energy building specifications. (2) Mediating process: Reduced embodied emissions from material production and construction activity, and sometimes reduced operational energy demand via efficiency-oriented design choices. (3) Outcome: Net reduction in CO₂e emissions over the building life cycle, dominated by avoided embodied emissions at build time (and potentially additional operational emissions reductions).
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced GHG emissions from building construction (especially embodied carbon in materials and construction processes) due to selecting lower-carbon project proposals.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: procurement-driven shift in construction design/material choices → lower embodied/operational carbon → direct reduction in GHG emissions.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: while “eco-efficiency” can overlap with resource efficiency, the mechanism’s climate-relevant endpoint is explicitly lower carbon footprint/emissions, which is a direct mitigation outcome.  
3) Edge cases applied: if “eco-efficiency” were implemented solely as reduced material use without verified GHG impact, it could resemble Resource Efficiency; however, the inclusion of carbon footprint criteria makes emissions reduction the dominant effect for this mechanism.  
4) Applicability to ALL instances: any policy using low-carbon procurement/selection criteria for construction projects aims to select lower-emission projects; the principal climate effect is mitigation via reduced construction (and sometimes operational) emissions.  
5) Note on subtypes: secondary subtype assignment (e.g., embodied vs operational carbon emphasis, specific materials, verification rigor) is deferred to policy-level evidence.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If procurement criteria prioritize general “eco-efficiency” without carbon accounting, classification could drift toward Resource Efficiency; conversely, if criteria primarily target climate resilience features (e.g., flood-proofing) with minimal emissions focus, it could become Adaptation—but that is outside this stated mechanism (carbon footprint criteria).
key_indicators: "plot conveyance competitions"; "based on quality"; "criteria will include the carbon footprint"; "eco-efficiency of the construction project"
co_benefits: Potential improvements in resource efficiency and reduced life-cycle costs/energy use, but not specified as primary aims in the text.
instance_edge_case_notes: Could be interpreted as an information/disclosure element if it only required reporting, but the phrasing indicates it is used as an evaluation criterion in competitions (i.e., procurement scoring), so classified as procurement-requirement.
